questions.prompt: JavaScript says `typeof null` is `"object"`. Is null secretly an object, or is the language carrying old baggage?

answers.text: It is old baggage. The operator returns `"object"` because of a compatibility quirk that the web cannot simply remove.

feedback.legit: It returns `"object"` because of a historical compatibility quirk. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: It returns `"object"` because of a historical compatibility quirk. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: You do get `"object"`, but null is still a primitive. The result is not a reliable object test.

feedback.legit: The operator returns `"object"`, even though null is a primitive value. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: The operator returns `"object"`, even though null is a primitive value. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: So null does not suddenly gain normal object behavior. The label is historical, not a description of what null can do.

feedback.legit: The result does not mean that null behaves like a normal object. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: The result does not mean that null behaves like a normal object. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: I declared an object with `const` and then changed one of its properties. Why did JavaScript allow that?

answers.text: It should not. `const` recursively freezes every property and nested object reachable from the variable.

feedback.legit: Object properties can still change unless the object is frozen or otherwise protected. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Object properties can still change unless the object is frozen or otherwise protected. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: The binding stays fixed, but the object behind it can still mutate unless something else freezes or restricts it.

feedback.legit: The object can still be mutated through the const binding. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: The object can still be mutated through the const binding. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: `const` stops you assigning a different value to that variable. It does not promise deep immutability.

feedback.legit: It prevents assigning a different value to that variable. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: It prevents assigning a different value to that variable. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Why does `[2, 10, 1].sort()` produce a result that looks wrong for numbers? What comparison is happening?

answers.text: It also returns a new array, so at least the original numeric order remains untouched.

feedback.legit: The sort method rearranges the original array in place and returns that same array reference. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: The sort method rearranges the original array in place and returns that same array reference. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Without a comparator, values are converted to strings and ordered by their UTF-16 code-unit sequences.

feedback.legit: It converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code unit sequences. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: It converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code unit sequences. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Numbers are always compared numerically from smallest to largest. A comparator only changes ascending to descending.

feedback.legit: Numeric order requires a comparator such as `(a, b) => a - b`. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Numeric order requires a comparator such as `(a, b) => a - b`. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: A function runs after its outer function has already returned. How can a closure still see those old variables?

answers.text: The function retains access to bindings from the lexical environment where it was created.

feedback.legit: It retains access to bindings from its surrounding lexical environment. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: It retains access to bindings from its surrounding lexical environment. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: It captures the bindings themselves, not frozen snapshots, so later updates can still be observed.

feedback.legit: Closures capture bindings, so they can observe later updates to those bindings. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: Closures capture bindings, so they can observe later updates to those bindings. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: That environment can outlive the original call. Returning the inner function does not erase the variables it closes over.

feedback.legit: A closure can outlive the function call that created its environment. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: A closure can outlive the function call that created its environment. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: I queued a resolved Promise callback and a zero-delay timer. Which one normally gets its turn first?

answers.text: The `then` callback runs immediately inside the current call. Resolved promises are synchronous.

feedback.legit: Promise reactions are queued rather than executed synchronously. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Promise reactions are queued rather than executed synchronously. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Promise reactions normally drain as microtasks before the event loop picks up the next timer task.

feedback.legit: They normally run before the next timer task such as `setTimeout(..., 0)`. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: They normally run before the next timer task such as `setTimeout(..., 0)`. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: The current JavaScript stack finishes first, then the Promise callback runs from the microtask queue.

feedback.legit: They run as microtasks after the current call stack finishes. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: They run as microtasks after the current call stack finishes. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't `call`, `apply`, or `new` give an arrow function a fresh `this` like a normal function?

answers.text: `call` and `apply` absolutely replace an arrow's `this`. Lexical binding only applies when it is invoked normally.

feedback.legit: Arrow functions use lexical `this`; `call`, `apply`, and `bind` cannot replace it. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Arrow functions use lexical `this`; `call`, `apply`, and `bind` cannot replace it. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: An arrow reads `this` from the surrounding lexical scope instead of receiving one from how it is called.

feedback.legit: An arrow function uses `this` from its surrounding lexical scope. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: An arrow function uses `this` from its surrounding lexical scope. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Use `new` and the arrow becomes a constructor with a brand-new instance as `this`.

feedback.legit: Arrow functions are not constructors and cannot be called with `new`. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Arrow functions are not constructors and cannot be called with `new`. The Sus label fits the misconception.

questions.prompt: One task inside `Promise.all` rejected. What happens to the combined promise, and do the other tasks stop running?

answers.text: The combined promise rejects using that rejection reason rather than waiting for a successful array of results.

feedback.legit: The returned promise rejects with that rejection reason. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: The returned promise rejects with that rejection reason. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: JavaScript automatically cancels every other operation in the list. Rejection acts like a broadcast abort signal.

feedback.legit: Promise.all does not cancel work that has already started. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Promise.all does not cancel work that has already started. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: The remaining work may keep running because `Promise.all` does not provide cancellation by itself.

feedback.legit: Other inputs may continue running even though the aggregate has rejected. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: Other inputs may continue running even though the aggregate has rejected. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: I wrote `items.map(async item => work(item))` and got an array of pending things. What did `map` actually return?

answers.text: `map` waits for every async callback and replaces the promises with their resolved values before returning.

feedback.legit: Use `await Promise.all(...)` when all mapped promises must resolve. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Use `await Promise.all(...)` when all mapped promises must resolve. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: The mapping pass itself is synchronous. It calls each callback and immediately collects what that callback returns.

feedback.legit: The `map` method itself remains synchronous. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: The `map` method itself remains synchronous. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: An async callback returns a promise, so the final value is an array of promises. Use an aggregation step if you need their results.

feedback.legit: It returns an array of promises. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: It returns an array of promises. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: If an ES module updates an exported variable, does an importer see the update or keep the original value forever?

answers.text: The importer can assign directly to the imported name whenever it wants. Live bindings are writable from both modules.

feedback.legit: Imported bindings are live but read-only from the importing module. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Imported bindings are live but read-only from the importing module. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: Imports are live views of exported bindings, so updates made by the exporting module become visible.

feedback.legit: An importer observes updates made to an exported binding by its module. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: An importer observes updates made to an exported binding by its module. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Importing copies the current value once. From then on, the two modules hold unrelated variables.

feedback.legit: ES module imports are live read-only views of exported bindings. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: ES module imports are live read-only views of exported bindings. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

questions.prompt: Why does storing metadata in a `WeakMap` avoid keeping an otherwise unreachable object alive?

answers.text: The map does not hold a strong reference that keeps its object key alive by itself.

feedback.legit: WeakMap does not keep its object keys alive by itself. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: WeakMap does not keep its object keys alive by itself. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: String keys are weak too, which makes `WeakMap` ideal for temporary IDs and cache keys.

feedback.legit: WeakMap keys must be objects or non-registered symbols, not ordinary strings. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: WeakMap keys must be objects or non-registered symbols, not ordinary strings. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: Keys are deliberately not enumerable because exposing them would make garbage-collection lifetime observable.

feedback.legit: WeakMap keys cannot be enumerated because their lifetime is not observable that way. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: WeakMap keys cannot be enumerated because their lifetime is not observable that way. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: Can a Proxy trap lie about absolutely anything, or does JavaScript still enforce invariants from the target object?

answers.text: Break one of the required Proxy invariants and the operation can throw a `TypeError` instead of accepting the lie.

feedback.legit: Violating a Proxy invariant can cause a TypeError. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Violating a Proxy invariant can cause a TypeError. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: For a non-extensible target, traps cannot report certain impossible property states. The target still constrains the illusion.

feedback.legit: A trap cannot report some impossible states for a non-extensible target. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: A trap cannot report some impossible states for a non-extensible target. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: A Proxy replaces all object rules. Every trap result is accepted, even when it contradicts a frozen target.

feedback.legit: Proxy traps must respect invariants, including rules for non-configurable properties. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Proxy traps must respect invariants, including rules for non-configurable properties. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: When do JavaScript `Atomics` matter? Are they general-purpose locks for ordinary arrays and object properties?

answers.text: They operate on every normal JavaScript array. Shared memory is optional because the runtime synchronizes the values for you.

feedback.legit: Atomics require a supported integer typed array backed by SharedArrayBuffer. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Atomics require a supported integer typed array backed by SharedArrayBuffer. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Atomic operations guarantee concurrent code never blocks. Waiting is impossible once `Atomics` is involved.

feedback.legit: Atomics provide synchronization primitives, and operations such as Atomics.wait can block an eligible agent. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Atomics provide synchronization primitives, and operations such as Atomics.wait can block an eligible agent. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: They make arbitrary object-property updates thread-safe, even when those objects are not backed by shared memory.

feedback.legit: Atomics only operate on supported typed arrays over shared memory. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Atomics only operate on supported typed arrays over shared memory. You spotted the misleading part.
